full.stage1:
```json
{
  "bottleneck_type": "memory-bound",
  "data_dependencies": [
    "The computation of B[i][j] depends on A[i][j], A[i][j-1], A[i][j+1], A[i+1][j], and A[i-1][j].",
    "The computation of A[i][j] in the next step depends on B[i][j], B[i][j-1], B[i][j+1], B[i+1][j], and B[i-1][j]."
  ],
  "parallelization_challenges": [
    "The kernel exhibits a stencil pattern, which has inherent data dependencies between neighboring elements.",
    "Boundary conditions need to be handled carefully to avoid out-of-bounds memory access.",
    "The two-step update process (A to B, then B to A) requires synchronization between steps."
  ],
  "recommended_strategy": "Use a 2D grid of threads where each thread computes one element of B or A. Employ shared memory to cache neighboring elements to reduce global memory accesses. Synchronize threads at the end of each time step to ensure all updates are completed before proceeding to the next step.",
  "gpu_suitable": true
}
```
full.stage2:
```json
{
  "validation": "partial",
  "corrected_analysis": {
    "bottleneck_type": "memory-bound",
    "why_memory_bound": [
      "Per interior point update: ~5 global loads + 1 global store (double). That is ~48 bytes moved (5*8 + 1*8) for ~6 FLOPs (5 adds + 1 mul), i.e., ~0.125 FLOP/byte -> strongly bandwidth-limited on modern GPUs.",
      "Two sweeps per time step doubles traffic; arithmetic intensity remains low."
    ],
    "data_dependencies": [
      "Within a single sweep (A->B), each B[i][j] depends only on A values from the previous time step/sweep; there are no write-after-read hazards within the same sweep because B is written once and A is read-only.",
      "Between sweeps (A->B then B->A), there is a global dependency: the second sweep must see all B results from the first sweep for the same time step."
    ],
    "parallelization_challenges": [
      "Main challenge is memory bandwidth and redundant global loads due to stencil reuse; shared memory tiling (or cache-friendly access) is key.",
      "Boundary handling: only update i,j in [1, n-2]; boundaries can be left unchanged or handled separately.",
      "Synchronization requirement is only between the two sweeps (and between time steps). This is naturally provided by launching separate kernels (global barrier). A per-block __syncthreads() is insufficient for the whole grid."
    ],
    "notes_on_previous_analysis": [
      "Correct that it is memory-bound and stencil-based.",
      "Incomplete/incorrect implication: 'inherent data dependencies between neighboring elements' within a sweep is misleading—neighbors are read-only, so the sweep is embarrassingly parallel over (i,j). The true dependency is only across sweeps/time steps.",
      "Correct that shared memory can help; must be described as tiling with halo."
    ]
  },
  "gpu_strategy": {
    "approach": "Two-kernel-per-timestep Jacobi with 2D tiling + shared-memory halo (ping-pong A/B). Optionally fuse the two sweeps using cooperative groups (grid sync) if supported, but default is separate kernels.",
    "description": [
      "Map the interior (n-2)x(n-2) points to a 2D CUDA grid. Each thread computes one output point.",
      "Use shared memory to stage a tile of the input array plus a 1-cell halo on all sides (tileDim+2 by tileDim+2). This reduces global loads from ~5 per point toward ~1 per point (amortized), limited by halo overhead.",
      "Launch kernel1: compute B = stencil(A). Launch kernel2: compute A = stencil(B). Repeat for tsteps.",
      "Keep A and B resident on device for all time steps; only copy back at the end."
    ],
    "kernel_design": {
      "thread_block_shape": "Use 2D blocks, e.g., (32,8) or (16,16). Prefer x dimension multiple of 32 for coalescing. Tune based on occupancy and shared memory.",
      "indexing": [
        "Global indices: i = blockIdx.y*blockDim.y + threadIdx.y + 1; j = blockIdx.x*blockDim.x + threadIdx.x + 1;",
        "Only compute if i <= n-2 and j <= n-2."
      ],
      "shared_memory_tiling": {
        "tile": "shared double sh[tileY+2][tileX+2]",
        "loads": [
          "Each thread loads its center element into sh[ty+1][tx+1].",
          "Threads on block borders additionally load halo cells (left/right/top/bottom).",
          "Four corner halo elements loaded by 4 threads (or guarded loads).",
          "__syncthreads() after loads."
        ],
        "compute": "out = 0.2*(sh[ty+1][tx+1] + sh[ty+1][tx] + sh[ty+1][tx+2] + sh[ty][tx+1] + sh[ty+2][tx+1])",
        "store": "Write to output array at [i][j]."
      },
      "boundary_handling": [
        "Do not launch/compute for boundaries (i=0,n-1 or j=0,n-1).",
        "Alternatively, allow threads to exist but guard with if and avoid out-of-bounds halo loads."
      ],
      "synchronization": [
        "Kernel launch boundary provides global synchronization between A->B and B->A sweeps and between time steps.",
        "Do not rely on __syncthreads() for inter-block sync."
      ],
      "optional_optimizations": [
        "Use __restrict__ pointers and linearized indexing (row-major) for better compiler optimization.",
        "Consider read-only cache (__ldg) on older architectures; on newer GPUs normal global loads are cached.",
        "If n is large, consider using CUDA graphs to reduce kernel launch overhead across many tsteps.",
        "If hardware supports cooperative launch and grid synchronization, you can fuse the two sweeps into one kernel per timestep with grid.sync() between sweeps; otherwise keep two kernels."
      ]
    }
  },
  "expected_speedup": "Typically large vs single-core CPU (often 10x-100x depending on CPU baseline and n,tsteps). Versus a naive GPU kernel without shared-memory tiling, tiling often yields ~1.3x-2.5x by reducing redundant global loads; exact gain depends on cache behavior, block size, and memory subsystem.",
  "implementation_notes": [
    "Use double precision; ensure GPU has adequate FP64 throughput, but kernel remains bandwidth-bound so FP64 rate is usually not the limiter.",
    "Allocate A_d and B_d once; copy A/B in, iterate tsteps on device, copy final result out.",
    "Prefer linear memory (double* with pitch or contiguous n*n) over VLA-style 2D arrays for CUDA; compute idx = i*n + j.",
    "Choose block sizes to balance shared memory usage and occupancy. Shared memory per block is (tileX+2)*(tileY+2)*8 bytes; e.g., 32x8 -> 34*10*8 = 2720 bytes (small).",
    "Grid dimensions: dim3 grid(ceil((n-2)/tileX), ceil((n-2)/tileY)).",
    "If boundaries must remain constant, do not write them; if they must be updated differently, run a separate boundary kernel.",
    "Validate correctness with small n and tsteps; compare against CPU reference."
  ]
}
```
